questions.question: Encrypt the plaintext 'ATTACK' using the Vigenere cipher with keyword 'KEY'. Show your work.
keyPoints: Keyword repeats as K-E-Y-K-E-Y, corresponding to shifts 10-4-24-10-4-24.

questions.question: Explain why the Vigenere cipher is more secure than a monoalphabetic cipher but can still be broken. Describe the complete cryptanalysis process.
keyPoints: The Vigenere cipher uses multiple substitution alphabets (one per keyword letter), so the same plaintext letter encrypts to different ciphertext letters at different positions.
This flattens the overall frequency distribution, defeating simple single-letter frequency analysis.
However, the keyword repeats cyclically, creating patterns when the same plaintext segment aligns with the same keyword position.
Step 1: Use the Kasiski Examination to find repeated sequences in ciphertext and compute GCD of their distances to determine keyword length.
Step 2: Split the ciphertext into groups based on keyword length; each group is a simple Caesar cipher.
Step 3: Apply frequency analysis to each group independently to determine each shift value and reconstruct the keyword.

questions.question: What is the Index of Coincidence and how is it used to determine the keyword length in Vigenere cipher cryptanalysis?
keyPoints: The IC measures the probability that two randomly chosen letters from a text are identical.
English text has IC around 0.065; random/uniformly distributed text has IC around 0.038.
By splitting ciphertext into groups of size n (testing different keyword lengths), the correct n produces groups with IC close to 0.065.
The keyword length n that yields the highest average IC across groups is most likely correct.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between the Vigenere cipher and the One-Time Pad. Why is the OTP theoretically unbreakable while the Vigenere is not?
keyPoints: The One-Time Pad is a special case of the Vigenere cipher where the keyword is as long as the plaintext.
Additionally, the OTP key must be truly random (not a meaningful word) and used only once.
The Vigenere cipher is breakable because the keyword repeats, creating statistical patterns that the Kasiski Examination and IC analysis exploit.
The OTP has no repeating pattern because the key never cycles; every ciphertext letter is shifted by an independent random value.
Shannon proved the OTP provides perfect secrecy: for any ciphertext, every possible plaintext is equally likely, giving the attacker zero information.
